column9: Track content: I uploaded the very first track in the Hi-Audio Radiophrenia 2026 Jam session called "Nela_Brown_Paris_Place_de_la_Concorde_.Traffic_short_2025-11-18.wav". I recorded the track using the Sennheiser Ambeo In-Ear Smart Headset. The binaural recording is perceived as stereo when listened to via speakers or as immersive when listened to via headphones.

column31: The interface was very easy to understand. I did not need to read a manual or watch any videos to find the menus/functionality after a short onboarding process.
column32: The 'transport bar' did not provide very clear feedback when buttons were pressed (i.e. buttons did not change colour), so it was difficult to see if something was happening or not (visually). Some of the icons in the interface were confusing. For example, hovering over the 'Headphone' icon shows text 'select cursor' which is confusing as it is very different to DAW. Clicking Mute and Solo buttons was also confusing. The empty track, being depicted with deep blue colour, looked more like some sort of error than the track being empty. Sometimes it took a long time for the track to be 'rendered', so I had to keep Firefox open until that was completed (whilst getting on with my other tasks).
column33: Hi-Audio platform would be very useful in remote music education. For example, imagine a child living in the French countryside and wanting to learn the saxophone. Issues: there are no music schools nearby; they are too young to go on the train by themselves, their parents can not drive them to the big city on a weekly basis. Now imagine that a music school in a big city (like Paris) has a curriculum that can be delivered online. For example, a teacher can record introductory videos about different types of saxophones and demonstrate a few things, like finger placements and embouchure. They can then record a simple scale, upload sheet music for the scale, and the child can play along with this recording to practice. When they think they have practised enough, they can record a track. The teacher will review it, give them feedback via video and give them the next exercise. Teachers could organise some in-person sessions with the student, but these would be complementary to the online session. Child and teacher could also review their progress by going back in time to review their historic sessions, and the child can always go back in time to review video lesson content. I understand this is a completely different view of how the platform could be used, and would involve changes to the platform, but it is worth thinking about.
